title: Personal Experience with AI Agents for Trading – Which One Actually Works?
author: Actual_Photo_690
selftext:
Hey everyone,

Curious about your real-world experience using AI assistants for trading-related tasks — portfolio analysis, stock research, news interpretation, earnings reports, etc.

I'm currently using \*\*Claude Sonnet 4.6\*\* and I'm reasonably satisfied, but I keep seeing people integrate chatbots directly into Robinhood, and it seems like \*\*ChatGPT + Grok\*\* are getting better results in those setups, with Claude apparently ranking near the bottom for this use case.

A few specific questions:

- Which AI agent do you actually use for portfolio analysis, stock potential, and news sentiment — and why?
- Has anyone noticed a meaningful difference between \*\*ChatGPT Free vs. Plus/Pro\*\* for financial analysis specifically?
- Is Grok genuinely better for markets given its real-time X/Twitter feed access?
- Any experience combining multiple AI tools (e.g. Gemini for news, Claude for fundamentals)?

Not looking for "AI can't predict the market" disclaimers — I know that. Just interested in which tools give the most useful, actionable insights in your workflow.

Thanks

comments.author: ripmetoopieces
comments.body: I decided to go with a trading agent bot built by ex Wall Street analyst to not mess with it myself

comments.author: PennyRoyalTeeHee
comments.body:
I run a weekly report that takes COT data and current economic data from the major 8 currencies. 

I have the report provide an aggregate score of bull/bear for each major fiat currency pair and provide outlook based on historical data and current events.

Once Opus 4.7 was released and I started testing, the report started being more important for me. 

Sonnet was not great at reasoning through complexities and just wouldn’t check its own logic.

I fed the data into ChatGPT5.5 recently - underwhelming results with just a lot of fluff in its output (instructions are specific to be concise in any prose).

I gave Gemini pro a spin and it had trouble handling the output (but this was once, I just didn’t see a reason to waste time when I have something that works).

comments.author: Outrageous_Aspect919
comments.body: Here’s my experience with some details in each report. Navigate to the website below, click on the printer and daily report. Agenttradingfloor.com

comments.author: AttitudeGrouchy33
comments.body:
Founder of milo here. My view after building in this space: the specific model matters less than the workflow you wrap around it.

Claude/GPT/Grok can all be useful for market reasoning, but none of them should be treated as the account brain by themselves. They do not natively know your positions, risk limits, cash, tax constraints, or whether the data they are reading is stale.

The best setups I’ve seen separate the system into layers:

- model for research/reasoning
- deterministic state for positions and exposure
- risk layer that can reject trades
- execution layer that does not improvise
- audit log so you can judge decisions later

That’s the lesson behind what we’re building with milo: don’t just ask “which AI is smartest?” Ask “which agent can I inspect, constrain, compare, and fire if it underperforms?”

If useful, this is what we’re working on: https://app.andmilo.com/?code=@milo4reddit
